name: master aglio e olio
instructions:
Three variants of the italian oil-and-garlic pasta shape. Variant A is the canonical classic; variants B and C are vegetable-forward extensions that use the same garlic-in-olive-oil base as their flavor engine.

=== VARIANT A — CLASSIC AGLIO E OLIO ===

The canonical form. Minimum-viable italian pasta — 5 ingredients plus salt and pasta water.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1/3 to 1/2 cup good olive oil
- 6-8 cloves garlic, thinly sliced (not minced)
- red chili flakes (a generous pinch)
- fresh parsley, roughly chopped
- pasta (traditionally spaghetti or linguine — long strands work here)
- salt (for the pasta water, generous)

PROCEDURE:
1. Boil pasta in well-salted water until just al dente. Reserve a generous cup of pasta water before draining.
2. Meanwhile, warm the olive oil over LOW heat in a wide pan. Add the sliced garlic and red chili flakes. Cook gently until the garlic is fragrant and just barely golden — never brown. Burnt garlic ruins the dish; low and slow is the only technique.
3. Add the drained pasta to the garlic oil along with a splash of pasta water. Toss vigorously to emulsify the oil and water into a light sauce.
4. Off heat, fold in the chopped parsley. Salt to taste.

=== VARIANT B — GARLICKY BROCCOLI ===

Same aglio e olio base, with broccoli as the vegetable centerpiece.

INGREDIENTS:
- olive oil (generous)
- 6-8 cloves garlic, sliced
- red chili flakes
- 1 head of broccoli, cut into small florets (include the tender stems, peeled if woody)
- fresh parsley (optional here — broccoli is doing the green work)
- pasta (spaghetti or short pasta both work)
- salt

PROCEDURE:
1. Boil broccoli florets briefly in the pasta water (2-3 min) before or with the pasta, so they turn bright green and just tender. Fish out with a spider.
2. Cook pasta to al dente in the same water. Reserve pasta water.
3. Warm olive oil over low heat with sliced garlic and chili flakes.
4. Add drained broccoli and pasta to the pan with a splash of pasta water. Toss to combine and lightly emulsify.
5. Salt to taste. Optional parsley finish.

=== VARIANT C — GARLICKY GREEN BEANS ===

Same base, green beans as the vegetable. Green beans hold more structure than broccoli and give a different textural contrast.

INGREDIENTS:
- olive oil (generous)
- 6-8 cloves garlic, sliced
- red chili flakes
- fresh green beans, trimmed and cut into 1-inch lengths (or left whole)
- fresh parsley
- pasta (short pasta shapes hold the beans better than spaghetti)
- salt

PROCEDURE:
1. Boil green beans briefly in the pasta water (~3-4 min) until just tender but still with bite. Fish out.
2. Cook pasta to al dente in the same water. Reserve pasta water.
3. Warm olive oil over low heat with garlic and chili flakes.
4. Add drained green beans and pasta to the pan with a splash of pasta water. Toss to combine.
5. Fold in parsley. Salt to taste.
notes:
MINIMUM-VIABLE ITALIAN PASTA: variant A is the italian pantry-pasta shape at its most minimal — olive oil, garlic, chili, parsley, pasta, salt. Nothing to hide behind, which is the whole point. Every ingredient has to be good, and the technique has to be right.

BURNT GARLIC IS THE #1 FAILURE MODE: this dish lives or dies on the garlic bloom. Cook it LOW and SLOW in the olive oil — the goal is fragrant, just barely golden garlic. Brown or burnt garlic makes the entire dish taste acrid and there's no rescuing it. If you burn the garlic, throw it out, wipe the pan, start over. This is the single most important technique note.

SLICED GARLIC, NOT MINCED: thin slices give you the surface area to release flavor into the oil without going to paste — and they don't burn as fast as minced. Minced garlic goes from raw to burnt in seconds; sliced garlic gives you a wider window.

GENEROUS OLIVE OIL — GOOD QUALITY: this is a dish where the olive oil is the sauce, so quality matters. Peppery, grassy, real EVOO — not a neutral cooking oil.

PASTA WATER IS LOAD-BEARING: aglio e olio has no other emulsifier. The starchy pasta water is what lets the oil cling to the noodles instead of pooling at the bottom of the bowl. Reserve more than you think you need; toss vigorously to emulsify.

LONG STRANDS FOR VARIANT A, FLEXIBLE FOR B/C: variant A traditionally uses spaghetti or linguine — the long strands catch the oil and the sauce coats them evenly. Variants B and C can go either way; short pasta actually holds the broccoli or green beans better than spaghetti does.

=== VARIANT B / C NOTES ===

BROCCOLI AND GREEN BEANS EACH BRING THEIR OWN TEXTURE: broccoli gives more surface area, more chew per bite, and its florets catch the garlic oil in their crevices. Green beans stay more discrete — long-and-crunchy against the pasta — and read cleaner and less rustic. Pick based on which texture register you want.

COOK THE VEGETABLES IN THE PASTA WATER: don't sauté them separately. Blanching in the pasta water saves a pan, keeps their color bright, and starts the pasta-water starch working for both the vegetable AND the pasta.

CHEESE — NOT CANONICAL, BUT NOT WRONG: aglio e olio traditionally skips cheese — the dish is meant to be a lean oil-and-garlic pasta. Some people grate parmesan or pecorino on top; it's not wrong but it's not the classic form. If you want cheesy pasta, this isn't the recipe to modify — go make something else.

HEAVY CREAM IS SECONDARY, NOT CANONICAL: a splash of heavy whipping cream at the end will work — it turns the dish into something creamier and richer, and it's not offensive. But it's a departure from the canonical form. Real aglio e olio is oil + starch water as the entire liquid; cream changes the mouth register entirely. Use if you want, know that you're stepping off the traditional path.

SUNFLOWER SEEDS ARE PRIMARY — BUT DON'T CRUSH THEM: toasted sunflower seeds scattered on top add a subtle nutty crunch that pairs cleanly with the garlic oil. Non-negotiable precondition: DO NOT CRUSH THEM. Whole toasted seeds only — never grind them into dust, and never process them with breadcrumbs. Pine nuts tolerate being crushed to dust with breadcrumbs (see pine nut breadcrumbs composite); sunflower seeds do NOT. Crushed sunflower seeds turn the dish CHALKY — a gritty dry-mineral mouthfeel that the light oil-and-pasta base can't hide. Same underlying property as the sunflower-seeds-in-red-pesto clash (see master-italian-tomato-sauce-and-pesto). Whole seeds, scattered late, no processing.

=== WHERE THIS TURNS INTO PASTA CON LE SARDE (SICILIAN SARDINE PASTA) ===

IF YOU START ADDING FISH, BREADCRUMBS, PINE NUTS, RAISINS, LEMON, AND/OR TOMATO — YOU'RE MAKING SARDINE PASTA: aglio e olio and pasta con le sarde share the same starting point (oil + garlic + chili + long pasta) but they diverge quickly. Once you add ANY of the following, you're on the sardine-pasta branch, not aglio e olio anymore:
- CANNED SARDINES or ANCHOVIES
- TOASTED BREADCRUMBS (topping)
- PINE NUTS
- RAISINS
- LEMON (juice or zest)
- FRESH TOMATOES
- FENNEL BULB or FENNEL SEEDS (whole, textural)
Note that FENNEL POWDER (ground) is welcome in the aglio e olio base itself — a small pinch adds background anise depth without pushing the dish into sardine territory. It's the whole-fennel forms (bulb, seeds) that carry the sicilian sardine signature.
Sardine pasta is the more complicated expression — it's got the same DNA but with layered sicilian sweet-sour-salty character. See sardine-parsley-pasta for that recipe. Don't try to combine — if you're reaching for the sardines and pine nuts, commit to that dish; if you want the clean oil-and-garlic register, keep this one lean.
